headline: HOGS RUN 11%, HORMUZ FLARES AGAIN, CROP PROGRESS DUE
subheadline: Lean hogs had their biggest single-session gain in months while Iran re-escalation scrambles the crude trade and USDA Crop Progress drops at 3 PM CT.
lead: Lean hogs ran 10.8% to $94.78, the biggest single-session move in this complex all summer, and nearby corn fell sharply 3.2% to $4.38 while December corn added 1.9% to $4.61 -- that gap is the market telling you it trusts the crop more than it trusts the calendar. Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now re-escalating after a weekend of U.S. strikes and Iranian claims of Hormuz closure, reversed crude's month-long deflation: WTI at $71.41 is down 1.4% on the day after giving back an early 4% gain as traders sorted signal from noise. USDA Crop Progress drops at 3 PM CT today, and with nearby corn already pricing crop confidence, a condition rating above 65% good/excellent keeps the nearby under pressure; a drop below 60% puts the weather premium back on the table.
the_takeaway: Hogs, Hormuz, and crop progress: three separate stories, all with resolution today or this week.
teaser: Hogs ran 11%, Iran flared again, and corn's nearby/December gap is the crop confidence trade in real time.

one_number.context: December corn closed $4.61 while nearby dropped to $4.38 -- a 23-cent spread that is the market's live poll on 2026 crop size. When nearby trades at a steep discount to new crop, the funds and commercials are saying the old-crop pipeline is adequately supplied and the weather premium belongs to the fall contract, not this one. Watch whether today's Crop Progress print widens or breaks down this gap: a condition decline pressures December higher without necessarily rescuing nearby.

yesterdays_call.summary: The call on July 10 was hogs down, toward $90.00, on the thesis that two weeks of fund selling had room to extend into the week.
yesterdays_call.note: Hogs closed $94.775 today -- not only did price never test $90.00, it ran 10.8% the wrong direction. The short-squeeze and tight cash market overwhelmed the bearish setup entirely. Miss, clean and simple.

sections.title: Corn Splits Hard
sections.body: Nearby corn fell sharply to **$4.38**, off 14.5 cents, while December added 8.5 cents to **$4.61** -- a 23-cent gap that is not noise. The funds are rotating out of old-crop long positions on evidence the early-July crop is in better shape than a wet May suggested, and the USDA WASDE two days ago raised U.S. production on the back of the June acreage update. Old-crop pipeline is full enough that nobody needs to bid for nearby bushels. December is holding because pollination weather is the known unknown: the crop is good now, not necessarily at tassel. USDA Crop Progress at 3 PM CT today is the make-or-break for whether this nearby weakness deepens or stabilizes. **Above 65% good/excellent, nearby corn stays under pressure. Below 60%, the weather premium migrates back into the December contract and the gap narrows.**
sections.catalyst: WASDE raised U.S. corn production on June acreage update; old-crop stocks adequate.
sections.bottom_line: Old crop is priced for a good crop; December holds the weather premium. Progress report decides.
sections.farmer_action: Unpriced old-crop corn: no reason to chase sales below $4.40 nearby. If December holds above $4.58 after crop progress, that is your scale-sale window on new crop. A condition rating below 60% reopens the door toward $4.75 December.

sections.title: Hogs Run Hard
sections.body: Lean hogs moved to **$94.78**, up 10.8% -- the biggest single-session gain in the complex in months, and it needs a catalyst the news bucket only partially supplies. The pork industry's National Swine Health Strategy build, announced in recent days, is not a price mover. What IS moving hogs is the combination of: the prior week's reversal setting a low-base entry point for the funds, cash pork markets tightening into summer grilling demand, and a technical short-squeeze after two weeks of selling. **No single clean news driver justifies 10.8% in one session; this has fund short-covering and technical buying layered on top of genuinely firm cash.** The Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout, ongoing since May 19, is still removing roughly 2% of weekly slaughter capacity and that processing constraint is keeping pork supply tighter than the headline herd numbers suggest. At $94.78, hogs are extended. The seasonal doesn't normally price a move this large in a single July Monday.
sections.catalyst: Short-covering on fund re-entry; processing constraints from ongoing Cargill lockout supporting cash pork.
sections.bottom_line: Extended after 10.8% in one day; short-covering plus tight cash, not a new fundamental story.
sections.farmer_action: Producers with unhedged hogs: $94.78 is real money. Consider locking 25-30% of near-term production here; this move is more likely to retrace than extend.

sections.title: Cattle Splits, Milk Falls Hard
sections.body: Live cattle firmed 1.6% to **$235.20** while feeder cattle eased 0.5% to **$354.60**, a split that has been the persistent story of this complex since the ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout began May 19. Live cattle can firm when processing is constrained and box-beef cutouts hold; feeders soften because producers know the pipeline is backed up and placement economics get murkier when slaughter capacity is uncertain. CME Group's announcement of two new beef trim futures contracts is a structural positive for risk management across the supply chain, but that is a multi-month story, not today's. **Class III Milk dropped 8.0% to $15.67**, the day's sharpest percentage move among the livestock complex outside of hogs, and at the 34th percentile of its 52-week range it is yelling structural oversupply. No clean catalyst in the milk bucket beyond the broader dairy complex adjusting to summer flush.
sections.catalyst: Cargill lockout maintains processing-constrained live/feeder split; CME beef trim futures launch announced.
sections.bottom_line: Live cattle holding on processing constraint; milk at $15.67 is the quiet distress story nobody is talking about.

sections.title: Hormuz Re-Escalates, Crude Gives Back Gains
sections.body: Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now re-starting after a weekend of U.S. strikes on Iran and Iranian claims that the Strait is closed to traffic, sent Asian crude up 4% overnight. WTI opened hard and then gave most of it back, settling at **$71.41**, down 1.4% on the day. That reversal is the market saying: the Strait is not actually closed, tanker operators are running dark (switching off transponders) but moving, and Nigeria hitting a six-year production high at 1.5 million barrels per day is a supply offset. Natural gas eased to **$2.94**, down 2.3%, even as European TTF opened 3% higher on Hormuz LNG fears -- the U.S. market is partially insulated because U.S. domestic supply is not Hormuz-dependent. **For crop producers, the key number is diesel.** A sustained Hormuz closure would move diesel inputs meaningfully; one weekend of escalation followed by a partial reversal does not yet change fall harvest input cost math.
sections.catalyst: U.S. strikes on Iran over weekend; Iranian Hormuz closure claims; tanker transponders going dark; Nigeria output at six-year high partially offsetting.
sections.bottom_line: Hormuz re-escalated, crude opened hard, then surrendered gains. Not a lasting input cost shock yet.
sections.farmer_action: Producers booking fall diesel: today's giveback is an opportunity. If WTI holds below $74 this week, current basis-adjusted diesel prices are reasonable to lock for harvest. A second escalation event would change that math quickly.

sections.title: Wheat and Beans Hold Firm
sections.body: Chicago wheat added 12.25 cents to **$6.32**, holding the WASDE-driven gains from last Thursday despite not reaching the $6.45 resistance that was the target for old-crop storage holders. The USDA's smaller U.S. crop estimate from the WASDE remains the foundation under the price, and old-crop stocks being down in the report gives merchandisers a reason to bid. Soybeans at **$11.96 nearby** and **$11.91 November** both firmed on fund and technical buying, consistent with the pattern from the prior week. **Soybean meal ran 2.4% to $323.10 and soybean oil ran 3.1% to $70.86**, the crush complex leading the flat price higher and telling you the processing side is doing the work, not export demand alone. Thursday's weekly export sales print will confirm whether that demand story has legs: under 300K MT soy, the chart is running on crush margin momentum without export support.
sections.catalyst: WASDE reduced U.S. wheat crop estimate; soybean crush margins leading nearby complex higher.
sections.bottom_line: Wheat at $6.32 is the WASDE floor holding; beans need export confirmation Thursday.
sections.farmer_action: Old-crop wheat in storage: $6.32 is still in range of the WASDE window. Scale additional sales on any close above $6.40. Carry costs are working against you if you wait past August.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC Settlement Locks In Right-to-Repair for John Deere Operators
outside_the_pit.body: The FTC's settlement with John Deere, confirmed this weekend, legally secures farmers' right to repair their own equipment using independent technicians and aftermarket parts. For producers carrying high debt loads into a compressed margin year, this means real dollars: authorized dealer repair markups have been running 40-60% above independent shop rates on major electronic systems. This is not a futures mover, but it changes the long-run cost structure for every row-crop operation.

outside_the_pit.title: Hormuz Tanker Traffic at Five-Week Low as Operators Go Dark
outside_the_pit.body: Tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz dropped to a five-week low Sunday as operators switched off transponders to avoid targeting by Iranian forces following the weekend U.S. strikes. Five carriers cleared the Strait during the week of May 25; this week that flow is already disrupted. If traffic stays suppressed into midweek, the crude market will re-price the Hormuz premium that spent most of June deflating.

outside_the_pit.title: Product of USA Label Gains 10 New Meat and Poultry Companies
outside_the_pit.body: USDA Secretary Rollins announced 10 additional meat and poultry companies joining the voluntary Product of USA labeling initiative, building domestic supply-chain transparency ahead of expected farm bill label provisions. For cattle and pork producers, broader adoption of the label strengthens the retail price premium argument for U.S.-origin product at a time when China's $17 billion annual U.S. ag purchase commitment through 2028 is keeping export demand in focus.

spread_to_watch.label: Corn nearby / December '26 carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: Nearby corn at $4.38 trading 23 cents under December $4.61 is the crop confidence spread in real time: the further this widens, the more the market is saying old-crop supply is adequate and new-crop risk belongs to weather, not stocks. Today's Crop Progress rating is the first real test of whether this spread holds or compresses -- a condition decline below 60% good/excellent would pull December higher and start closing this gap.

basis.headline: Old-crop corn basis softening; bean basis steady on crush demand.
basis.body: **Corn basis is widening across the Central Belt** as old-crop pipeline supplies remain ample and the WASDE production revision removes urgency from the merchandiser side. Producers east of the Mississippi are finding bids pulled back from last week's levels. Soybean basis is holding firm to slightly tighter in the Eastern Belt, where **crush plant demand is absorbing supply** and keeping local bids competitive. Wheat basis is mixed: elevator bids in the Southern Plains are firm on the WASDE crop-size reduction, while the Northern Plains basis has softened with harvest pressure building.

weekly_thread.question: Will USDA Crop Progress confirm the good-crop thesis that nearby corn is already pricing, or does a condition decline put the weather premium back on the board?
weekly_thread.status_text: Nearby corn falling sharply to $4.38 while December holds $4.61 tells you the market already voted for a good crop. Today's 3 PM CT Crop Progress report is the first data point that either validates that vote or forces a recount. This is the week's dominant question because the spread between old-crop and new-crop corn resolves around crop condition, not around macro noise.

the_more_you_know.title: The 23-cent corn spread is not a carry trade. It is a referendum-free crop poll.
the_more_you_know.body: When nearby corn trades 23 cents under December, as it does today at $4.38 versus $4.61, most producers read it as 'the market is in carry.' It isn't. Normal carry in corn is a function of storage rates and interest costs, roughly 4-6 cents per month. A 23-cent gap between nearby and a contract five months out implies something different: the market is assigning meaningfully lower demand urgency to old-crop bushels than to new-crop risk. The funds have been long new crop and short nearby since early July, not because they want to own storage, but because they believe the 2026 crop is large enough to relieve old-crop pressure while retaining a weather option on fall. Today's Crop Progress print will either tighten that spread (condition decline, new-crop risk rises) or widen it further (strong condition, old-crop pressure deepens). Producers with old-crop corn in storage should watch the spread, not just the flat price: if the gap narrows to 15 cents or less, the market is repricing crop risk upward and nearby bids will follow.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn condition above 65% good/excellent deepens nearby corn weakness and widens the nearby/December spread. Below 60% puts the weather premium back into December and starts closing the 23-cent gap.

Hormuz tanker traffic: if traffic stays at five-week lows through Wednesday, crude re-prices the Hormuz premium and WTI tests back above $74. That changes fall diesel input cost math for harvest planning.

Weekly export sales: soybeans under 300K MT means the crush-margin-led rally is running without export support and the chart is leading fundamentals. Above 400K MT confirms the China commitment starting to show in the weekly data.
Lean hogs follow-through: a close below $90.00 this week signals today's 10.8% move was a short-squeeze without fundamental follow-through. Above $97, the funds have re-established a real long.

Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout, ongoing since May 19: any resolution announcement immediately pressures live cattle and relieves the live/feeder split. No resolution reported as of this morning.

daily_quote.text: Don't put all your eggs in one basket.

source_summary: CME Group settlement prices; USDA WASDE July 2026; Brownfield Ag News; OilPrice.com; FarmPolicy News; USDA Crop Progress (pending 3 PM CT); Feedstuffs; Farm Doc Daily
